instructor.name: Khanh Nguyen
instructor.office_hours: W 2:00 - 3:00 PM or by appointment (via Google Calendar) in-person only

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Programming Languages. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the design space of programming languages via an in-depth study of two programming languages, one functional and one object-oriented; focuses on idiomatic uses of each language and on features characteristic for each language. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Technical Skills Expectations:
chunks.content: Students should be familiar with installing libraries/packages, configuring environments, executing the compilers, and running compiled programs (with or without an IDE). Familiarity with the Linux environment is needed. Experience with Git is expected.

chunks.title: Prerequisites Waiver:
chunks.content: Students who wish to enroll in the course without the prerequisites should contact the Undergraduate Advising Office at undergraduate-advisors@cse.tamu.edu, not the instructor.

chunks.title: Course Auditing:
chunks.content: Auditing a course must go through a formal approval process per student rules. This is often for very rare extenuating scenarios, i.e., the reason cannot be to prepare to take the class next semester, or to effectively retake a course without being enrolled.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the student will be able to:
• Use modern programming languages effectively
• Develop the necessary skills to learn and adopt new programming languages
• Evaluate the power of programming languages and their constructs
• Apply language constructs, common abstraction mechanisms, and efficiency considerations
• Analyze the meaning and efficiency of functional and object-oriented programs
• Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to build correct and elegant software
Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages, learning these languages should be considered a side benefit of the course, not the main goal.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The course grade is based on the following components:
• Homeworks: in forms of writing and/or coding: 5 assignments: 20% of grade. Each will carry a different weight.
• Quizzes: 4 online quizzes and unannounced, in-class pop quizzes: 20% of grade. Online quizzes are conducted online using Respondus Lockdown Browser. All quizzes are open-note only. Each will carry a different weight.
• Exams: 1 midterm exam (30% of grade), and 1 final (non-cumulative) exam (30% of grade). A double-sided 8.5×11 cheat sheet with font size no smaller than 11pt if typed, or handwritten, may be used in the exam.

Course Grading Scale: F < 50% ≤ D < 65% ≤ C < 78% ≤ B < 90% ≤ A

| F | D | C | B | A |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 25 | 50 | 65 | 78 | 90 | 100 |

• Final grade boundaries are determined by the lowest earned score in each grade bracket, not whole-numbered percentages. In other words, the minimum for any grade is defined by the score of the person at the bottom of that grade bracket. Decimal proximity to a round number does not result in an adjustment.

"No-Rounding" Cutpoint Logic

```
100
93.2
Cutoff for A
90
89.9
Still B:
below the person at 93.2%
80
78.5
70
60
50
A
B
65.1
52.8
C
D
Letter Grade
An example: each bar represents the
lowest-scoring person in each grade
bracket. If your final score is below a bar,
you are in the lower grade
```

• The grade assigned is not on a curve. In this course, no curve will be given.
• There will be no extra-credit assignments.
• The grades will be audited randomly at any point. This may result in increased/decreased scores for released grades. Affected students will be notified.

chunks.title: Class Participation:
chunks.content:
This course strictly enforces the University's Attendance Policy, which states
The University views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility.
Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.
No credit will be awarded for class attendance. If students miss class, it is their responsibility to catch up with the materials and announcements. The course staff will not repeat announcements that were made in class.

chunks.title: Attendance Certification:
chunks.content: As required by Federal aid regulations, the University requires an attendance certification of each student having begun engagement in an academically related activity. This course uses submissions of assignments, quizzes, homeworks as evidence to satisfy the required documentation. The attendance certification is due approximately at the end of the 3rd week. Students are hereby notified that by the due date - specifically 01/29/2026 5:00 PM, if students have zero submissions, they will not be certified of this course's attendance. There will be no warning or communication should a student be at risk of not being certified. Failure of this certification might negatively impact students' financial aid.

chunks.title: Regrading:
chunks.content:
Regrading can be requested for any graded materials. The guidelines are:
• The request must be made on Gradescope and within a specified window. Outside of this window, no requests will be accepted. No requests via email will be accepted. No exceptions will be made.
• The request must include a reasonable justification and/or documentation.
• Non-code submissions (e.g., writing assignments, exams): If the handwriting is not legible, what the graders guess is final, and the request will be denied.
• The following kinds of submissions will get 0 without grading, and regrading will be denied:
■ Disorganized, messy submissions.
■ Wrong submissions
■ Submissions that did not follow the instructions (e.g., submitting different file types, not matching the assignment outline with the Gradescope submission, using banned libraries).
■ Uncompilable programming assignments on Gradescope. “It works on my machine" argument will be ignored.

chunks.title: Makeup Exam Policy
chunks.content:
Unless due to excused absences, no makeup exam will be given. If an excused absence is expected, students should contact the instructor as soon as possible (with documentation). Makeup exams will be different from the original exams.
If a student misses an exam due to an excused absence, the student must take the makeup exam on the designated date and time:
• Midterm: Monday, 3/2/26 at 6:30 PM in PETR 226
• Final: Monday, 5/4/26 at 6:30 PM in PETR 226
Discuss with the instructor directly if rescheduling is required.

chunks.title: Incomplete Grade Policy
chunks.content:
Per Student Rule 10, the instructor shall give an incomplete grade only in cases due to an authorized absence or other causes beyond the control of the student, such as illness or personal emergency. No exceptions will be made. Students are required to furnish appropriate documentation. Under no circumstances can the "I" grade be given to a student who wishes to:
• Do additional work after the course deadline to improve a grade
• Redo the coursework in a subsequent semester to improve the current grade
Awarding an incomplete is always at the discretion of the instructor.

chunks.title: Deviations from the grading policy
chunks.content:
The grade for individual assignments and the course is assigned to each student based on concrete, quantifiable, objectively evaluated submissions and exams. Grade is not given to students. Hence, in the interest of fairness and consistency, requests for grade increases that are inconsistent with the stated grading scale are denied.
Here is an incomplete list of hypothetical requests:
• (I feel) I do not deserve [the grade/score] because of [reason(s)]
• (I feel) The (grading/course) rubric is unbalanced due to [reason(s)]
• I spend [huge number]% of my time on X which accounts for only [small number]% of the grade, [elaborate on how the grading should be here]
• I do very well on other assignments, but I panicked during tests
• With the grade lower than . . ., I will be [insert a consequence here]
• I worked very hard for the class, but because of [reason(s)] I failed
• I have too many other responsibilities.
• I am really close to getting a...
• I am willing to do extra work
• I have a good GPA so far
• I have never failed a class before.
• I have never taken a course in this area before
• I have never gotten a grade as low as . . . before
• It doesn't hurt to ask
• I am about to graduate.
• This course is too hard

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Submission of Assignments and Grade Posting:
• All assignments must be turned in electronically on Gradescope. Email submissions will not be accepted. Emails with submission attachments will be deleted without response. No exceptions will be made.
• Students are advised not to use Gradescope as a debugging tool, as the autograder might be buggy. Students, therefore, should focus on debugging locally.
• Grade posting: The score shown in Gradescope will be imported into Canvas as-is after the deadline (and regrading).

Assignment Deadlines and Late Penalty:
All assignments have established deadlines. Submitting a deliverable after the deadline is late work. Late submission is allowed but will incur a late penalty. No late work outside the grace period is accepted unless due to university-approved absences. The course staff will not answer assignment questions during the grace period.
• Should deadline discrepancies arise between the syllabus, assignment description PDF, and Gradescope: the deadline on Gradescope takes precedence and is the official deadline.
• Late submission will be graded as if it were on time, and then a late penalty will be deducted (out of full credit). Late penalty will be calculated as follows:
• Let h be the whole number of hours late (rounding up), the penalty is h²/24 + h. That is, a 12-hour-late assignment will receive -18% (out of 100% - full credit) as a penalty; 1 day late, -48%. At 36 hours late, the most one can score in an assignment is 10. At 39 hours late, the assignment receives no credit.
• See the graph below:

```
100
75
50
25
0
3 5
7
9
24.38
48.00
102.38
90.00
74.67
171131517 10 27 26 25 2 20 33 33 35 33 30
Hour
```

• Late penalty is waived in cases of university-approved absences
• Excused absences: Students are required to furnish documentation for an excused absence by default. When possible, notify the course staff of excused absences before the deadline - not after. Refer to Student Rule 7 for information about excused absences. This course will strictly enforce Rule 7.
• Computer crashes, malfunctions, and catastrophic loss of files are not valid excuses for not submitting work on time (and thus request for the waiver of the late penalty). Students are advised to turn in assignments early to avoid any issues due to emergencies.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Tentative Schedule
• OCP = Clarkson (chapter# in pdf, not the e-version)
• Dragon = Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman
• EaC = Cooper, Torczon
• Java = Schildt, Coward

Part I: OCaml

| Week | Topic | Readings |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Installing OCaml: will not have a lecture on this | OCP 2 |
| 1 | Basics: expressions, typing rules, functions, semantics | OCP 4.1-4.4, 4.6 |
| 2 | Unions and Products: tuples, records, patterns, options | OCP 5.2, 5.4, 5.6, 5.7 |
| 3 | List, Tail Recursion. | OCP 5.1, 5.5, 5.8 |
| 4 | Algebraic Data Types (ADT): simple and data-carrying variants, recursive and parameterized variants | OCP 5.9 |
| 5 | High Order Programming. Map & Filter: | OCP 6.1-6.3, 6.7 |
| 5 | High Order Programming. Fold | OCP 6.4, 6.5 |
| 6 | Variables, Scope, Parameter Passing, Closure | OCP 11.2, 11.4 |
| 7 | Type Checking, Type Inference | OCP 11.5, 11.6 |

Part II: Language Implementation
PL generals & front end

| Week | Topic | Readings |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | Lexical Analysis, Regular Expressions | Dragon 2.6, 3-3.4, 3.6-3.7, 3.8.3-3.8.4 EaC 2-2.4.3, 2.4.5, 2.5.3-2.5.4 |
| 8 | Syntactic Analysis, Context-Free Languages, Ambiguity | Dragon 2.2, 4-4.3 EaC 3-3.2, 3.5.3 |
| 9 | Spring Break | |
| 10 | Top-down Parsing | Dragon 2.4, 4.4 EaC 3.3 |
| 11 | Bottom-up Parsing | Dragon 4.5-4.8 EaC 3.4 |
| 11 | Lexer and Parser Generators: https://www.antlr.org/ | |

Part III: Java
Java topics

| Week | Topic | Readings |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | Java Basics: will not have a lecture on this | Java 3, 4, 5 |
| 12 | Class, Objects, and Methods | Java 6, 7, 13.13 |
| 13 | Interfaces, Multiple Inheritance. Dynamic Dispatch, Overloading, Overriding, Subtyping | Java 8, 9 |
| 14 | Threads | Java 11, 29 (concurrency utilities - skim) Tutorials on Java Concurrency |
| 15 | Java Generics | Java 14 Tutorials on Java Generics |
| 15 | Garbage Collection | GC readings mentioned in Materials |
| 16 | Reflection | Java 31 pages 1054-1057 Tutorials on Java Reflection |
| 16 | OOP vs. functional | Java 15, 17.1-17.3 |

Important Course Deadlines
• Quiz and homework dates are tentative and subject to change. So is the number of quizzes and homeworks.
• A practice quiz is available from 1/19/26 to 1/25/26.

chunks.title: Course Evaluation
chunks.content:
Evaluations are a way for students to provide feedback regarding their instructor and the course after the delivery, including student perceptions of learning and the student experience in the course. Detailed feedback will enable the instructor to continuously tailor and improve teaching methods and course content to meet the course's learning goals and the students' academic needs. They are a requirement of the course and are key to continuing to provide students with the highest quality of teaching.
Texas A&M University utilizes HelioCampus-Assessment, formerly AEFIS, to collect student course evaluations. The evaluations are anonymous; the instructor and administration do not track who entered what responses. At no point in the process that the instructor knows the students' identity or whether a student has completed the evaluation. Students are strongly encouraged to participate in the course evaluation. Refer to Office of Institutional Effectiveness & Evaluation page for more information. The instructor welcomes feedback during the course.
The Faculty Senate passed a Resolution in 2010 (FS.27.122) that specifically stated "The Faculty Senate opposes granting academic credit of any kind to students for completing course evaluations ...", this would include incentives such as bonus points. As such, there will be no credit for completing the course evaluation.

chunks.title: Accessibility Statement
chunks.content:
The instructor aims to create a learning experience that is as accessible and inclusive as possible. If a student anticipates or experiences any barriers to learning in this course, the instructor will consider creative solutions as long as they do not compromise the intent of the assessment or learning activities.
Students are strongly encouraged to contact Disability Resources and/or use the Disability Accommodation Portal (https://disability.tamu.edu/about/aim/). It is better if the students get accommodations and do not need them, rather than trying without accommodations and finding later that such accommodations are needed.

chunks.title: Professionalism and Respect
chunks.content: Students are expected to treat the instructor and all other participants in the course with courtesy and respect — refer to Student Rule 21. Comments to others should be factual, constructive, and free from harassment. Unprofessional or disrespectful conduct will not be tolerated. Warnings will not be given. Students will need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive ways within the course. Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions as specified within the Student Rules.

chunks.title: Recording Statement
chunks.content: Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording. This accommodation letter must be presented to the instructor in advance of any recording being done. Students with permission to record classes are not permitted to redistribute audio or video recordings of statements or comments from the course to other individuals without the express permission of the faculty member and of any students who are recorded.

chunks.title: Course Material Policy
chunks.content: Materials used in this course are subject to copyright. Students are not allowed to reproduce, distribute, or post any lecture materials, assignments, exams, solutions, etc., publicly. Students may take notes and make copies of course materials for personal use. Students may not post course materials on commercial websites such as Course Hero and Chegg. Doing so is a copyright violation and, in some cases, may also be an academic integrity violation that will be dealt with accordingly.

chunks.title: Plagiarism - Academic Integrity
chunks.content:
Plagiarism is one of the worst academic sins, for the plagiarist destroys trust among colleagues and the purpose of learning. Plagiarism is commonly defined as passing off as one's own ideas, words, writings, code, etc., which belong to another person. By this definition, in this course, a student commits plagiarism if copying the work elsewhere, even with the permission of the author, and turns it in as one's own.
The instructor encourages collaboration and the exchange of ideas among students. Collaboration is important for facilitating learning, and peers often can be a great resource. However, all assignments in this course are individual efforts. All assignments must be fully completed by only the student to whom they are assigned, without unauthorized aid. Any academic dishonesty will not be tolerated and will be severely dealt with. Consequences include a zero grade for the activity/assignment, course failure, and dismissal from the university, among others. Refer to the University's Academic Integrity Statement and Policy. All involved parties will be penalized equally.
• Specifically, students may discuss the assignments at a high level (e.g., concepts, approaches) with friends but absolutely no actual solution sharing (voluntarily or involuntarily) is allowed.
• Code: Students are not allowed to use code found elsewhere (e.g., Google searches, online repositories, past offerings of the course). Students also must not attempt to circumvent the objective of an assignment (e.g., using a banned library or a function to bypass the goal of the assignment)
• Non-code: The write-up of homeworks must be done individually and must not be shared in any way or form.
• No full or partial deliverables are allowed to be knowingly or unknowingly released in the public domain even after the deadlines and after the course is over. Students are solely responsible for securing all deliverables away from the public.
• Group review sessions for exams are allowed and encouraged.
• Posting assignments to or copying solutions from unauthorized sources is academic misconduct.
• There have been past incidents where students used code from Chegg or similar tutoring websites; the use of such resources is not allowed.

chunks.title: Complicity
chunks.content:
Every student should understand that complicity – helping or attempting to help another student commit an act of academic dishonesty – also constitutes academic dishonesty and carries the same punishment as cheating and plagiarism.
In other words, if providing a solution to another student, even if that student does not turn it in for credit, the provider has committed an act of academic dishonesty.
Occasionally, we find evidence of plagiarism after the assignments have been graded. For example, a prior assignment may be flagged when a new detector becomes available. For these reasons, you should treat your assignment grades as provisional. Any prior submission that is later found to constitute a violation will be reported to the Aggie Honor System Office (AHSO).

chunks.title: Use of Generative AI:
chunks.content:
The use of AI to gain a greater understanding of a topic is a great use of its capabilities. This is allowed. However, using AI to complete assignments is a poor use of its capabilities, as it does not increase a student's capabilities. This is prohibited.
• Code: While consulting AI tools is allowed, students are prohibited from submitting AI-generated code for the assignments.
• Writing: All non-code submissions must be generated by students themselves. Students must not have another entity write any portion of an assignment for them.

chunks.title: Aggie Honor Code
chunks.content:
Upon accepting admission to Texas A&M University, a student immediately assumes a commitment to uphold the Honor Code, to accept responsibility for learning, and to follow the philosophy and rules of the Honor System. Ignorance of the rules does not exclude any member of the Texas A&M University community from the requirements or the processes of the Honor System. By submitting anything to the course, electronically or otherwise, students acknowledge and assert the Honor Code, which states:
On my honor, as an Aggie, I have neither given nor received unauthorized aid on this academic work.
Often Honor Code violation is the result of panic when falling behind. Playing catch-up is never a good strategy when taking any courses, including this one. When in doubt about what constitutes a violation, students should contact the instructor. Do not engage in the “it is better to ask for forgiveness than permission” mentality.

chunks.title: Reporting
chunks.content: It is the responsibility of all members, including but not limited to students, staff, faculty, and teaching assistants of the Texas A&M University Community, to actively promote academic integrity. Instructors are expected to adhere to the policy pertaining to the reporting and adjudication of violations of the Aggie Code of Honor. Initiating formal procedures is a necessary and obligatory component of challenging and making known acts that violate the Aggie Code of Honor.

chunks.title: Fabrication
chunks.content: Any information (e.g., a doctor's note for an excused absence, sections in a project report) that is found to have been fabricated, falsified, or misused will be submitted to the Aggie Honor System Office (AHSO) with severe penalties, including but not limited to an F* for the course, or University expulsio,n which the instructor has used before

chunks.title: A Reminder of Rule 20.1.3
chunks.content: "Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case."

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content:
Campuswire
Campuswire will be the main communication and Q&A platform, not Canvas. The class link and code to join will be provided in the first class meeting. If you drop the class, please leave Campuswire. Interfering with or disrupting Campuswire will be reported and result in disciplinary actions according to Student Rules.
• Unless regarding personal matters (e.g., grade), questions are to be posted on Campuswire first, for the benefit of other students. Students who wish to discuss personal matters should see the course staff during office hours.

chunks.title: Campuswire
chunks.content:
• Students are encouraged to participate in the forum and help answer questions. Campuswire is mainly made for students by students.
• Students must never post codes or solutions on Campuswire for any reason. If asking for implementation clarification/guide, describe what has been done in pseudocode. Posts with codes/solutions will be deleted, and the poster might be banned from Campuswire.
• All posts to Campuswire must be public (visible to everyone) and un-anonymous. Private posts or messages to the course staff will be deleted.
• The order of getting answers is: Campuswire or in class, or office hours. Emailing should be last.
• Students must not treat Campuswire as a de-facto email system (e.g., make a post visible to TA and instructor only and discuss code/solution of the student). Those who violate will be banned from Campuswire.
• The course staff will not check Campuswire during non-working hours, e.g., weekends and late hours.
• In the past, students tended to wait until the due date and then panic and ask questions. This is ineffective and discouraged. To enforce better behavior, the course staff will not check Campuswire during the last 6 hours before the deadline.

chunks.title: Peer Teacher Central
chunks.content:
Aside from office hours and Campuswire, students can use Peer Teacher Central (PTC) as a learning resource. Students consistently rate peer teachers as having a positive impact on their academic performance. PTC is located in PETR 127. PTC has students who have completed this course and can help with explanation and clarification of concepts.
• Hours:
• Monday - Friday: 8:00 a.m. - 5:00 p.m. (in-person) | Discord after 5:00 p.m.
• Saturday: Closed
• Sunday: 2:00 - 5:00 p.m via Discord
• Link:
• Peer Teacher Central
https://engineering.tamu.edu/cse/academics/peer-teachers/index.html
• Discord server https://tx.ag/ptcdiscord

chunks.title: Respondus Lockdown Browser
chunks.content:
Some assessments will be conducted online using Respondus Lockdown Browser with Monitor - and will be referred to as quizzes. There is no cost to students to use the software. Students are required to have a webcam, which may be built into the computer or may be an external camera. The software is available for Mac, PC, and Chromebooks. It is important to test LockDown Browser and the webcam before using the application in a quiz. There will be an opportunity for students to practice and set up the environment. Students must ensure that they have a functional environment during the course to take quizzes. Unless due to extended excused absences, no makeup is possible.
Note: the "Available Until” date and time is a hard limit, triggering a submission automatically, and overriding any remaining time on the timer. Each quiz is not longer than 1 hour.
• University Library (Evans Annex) Tech Bar: Any Texas A&M student with an active NetID can check out electronics such as Chromebooks, chargers, and webcams from the Tech Bar for free. Equipment reservations are available up to seven days in advance. Walkups are also possible. For Chromebooks, use https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/chromebooksAnnex; for accessories, use https://tamu.libcal.com/reserve/computeraccessoriesAnnex
• Respondus should be available for use on these Chromebooks as a Chrome extension.
• Alternatively, students may be able to take Respondus Lockdown Browser exams in select computer labs on campus. See TAMU—Open Access Lab (OAL) Computer Workstation Locations (http://tx.ag/TAMUoal) for more information.

chunks.title: Course Announcements
chunks.content: Aside from Campuswire posts, the instructor will make class announcements via a mailing list: cs-csce314504-spring2026@lists.tamu.edu - not Canvas. Students should make sure they can receive emails from the mailing list. All emails to the mailing list are assumed to be read after 12 hours. Allow 24 hours after registering for the class for the roster to be synchronized with the mailing list. Past emails may be found on the Google Group page.

chunks.title: How to succeed in this course (or any course):
chunks.content:
Here are a few tips:
• Attend class meetings regularly.
• Take notes during lectures.
• Read the assigned readings before the lecture and your notes after class. Read the assigned readings again after class.
• Ask questions and participate in discussions in class and on Campuswire. Campuswire is FYBY - For You and By You.
• Practice, practice, practice. For each credit hour, you are expected to spend 3 hours per week outside of class. Allow sufficient time to absorb the materials.
• Study for exams. Group study/review for exams is allowed. Students in the past have found group review sessions and crowd-sourcing practice tests and review sheets helpful.
• Complete assignments on time.
• Visit the course staff during office hours to get questions answered.

boilerplate_policies: Civil Rights, Free Speech, and Title IX Policies
Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
Statement on the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
